---
title: "Solving $A\\mathbf{x}=\\mathbf{b}$: The Inverse, Singularity, and the No-Solution Case"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # generic vectors / data
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # the highlighted vector / target b
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # columns / solution / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"
PURPLE = "#6a4c93"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0), ls="-"):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0, linestyle=ls), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center", zorder=z + 1)
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the third article of **Movement III —
> Matrices as Maps**, the read-as-a-pair sibling of [GEOM-09 *Matrix Multiplication as
> Composition*] and [GEOM-10 *The Transpose*]. It assumes [GEOM-08 *Matrices as Linear
> Maps*] — the single idea that a matrix *does something to space*, and that $A\mathbf{x}$
> is a **linear combination of $A$'s columns** with the entries of $\mathbf{x}$ as the
> weights — and [GEOM-09], where $AB$ means "do $B$, then $A$" and the row-times-column rule
> fell out of that requirement. It leans on [GEOM-10] for the identities of the transpose,
> and on the span/subspace picture from [GEOM-02 *Span and Subspaces*]. Its job is to answer
> the most basic question you can ask about a linear map: *can you run it backwards?* When
> can we solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$, when can't we, and what does each verdict mean as a
> picture in space. It sets up [GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four Fundamental Subspaces*] (which
> names the spaces that decide the verdict), [GEOM-13 *The Determinant*] (the single number
> that flags singularity), [GEOM-19 *Cholesky*] and [GEOM-21 *The Pseudoinverse*] (the
> stable, always-defined replacements for the inverse), and it directly feeds **Regression
> R5**, where the object $(X^\top X)^{-1}$ is the whole game.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A linear system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ asks: *which input $\mathbf{x}$ does the map $A$
send to the target $\mathbf{b}$?* Because $A\mathbf{x}$ is a linear combination of $A$'s
columns, this is the same as asking *how to build $\mathbf{b}$ out of the columns of $A$*.
That single rephrasing settles everything. If the columns of $A$ reach $\mathbf{b}$ in
exactly one way, there is a unique solution and $A$ has an **inverse** $A^{-1}$ that undoes
it. If they cannot reach $\mathbf{b}$ at all — $\mathbf{b}$ lies outside the **column
space** — there is **no solution**, and the honest thing to do is settle for the closest
reachable point, which is the projection of [GEOM-06] and the least-squares problem of
Regression. If they reach $\mathbf{b}$ in more than one way, there are **infinitely many**
solutions, all differing by a vector the map crushes to zero (the **null space**). We
derive the inverse from Gaussian elimination, prove $(AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}$ and
$(A^\top)^{-1}=(A^{-1})^\top$, and tie the three outcomes to the geometry of the columns.
The single claim this article defends is that **solvability is not algebra to memorize but
a question about whether $\mathbf{b}$ lives in the span of $A$'s columns — and that the
practitioner's reflex "solve, don't invert" follows directly from understanding what the
inverse actually is.**

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ asks you to **write $\mathbf{b}$ as a combination of
the columns of $A$**. There are exactly three possible answers, and which one you get is
pure geometry:

- **Exactly one way** — $A$ is **invertible**, $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ is the unique
  solution, and the map can be run backwards.
- **No way at all** — $\mathbf{b}$ lies **outside the column space**; there is *no*
  solution, and the best you can do is the closest reachable point (projection /
  least squares).
- **More than one way** — the map has a nontrivial **null space**; there are *infinitely
  many* solutions, all differing by a direction $A$ collapses to $\mathbf{0}$.

The inverse exists if and only if the columns are a basis: they span the whole space and
do so without redundancy. Everything else in this article is a consequence of that one
sentence.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The symbol $A^{-1}$ will look like an algebraic gadget
you compute by row-reduction, but by the end you should see it as the *name for "the map
$A$ run backwards,"* an operation that exists only when running it backwards is
unambiguous. And you should leave with a working practitioner's instinct: on a computer you
almost never *form* $A^{-1}$, you *solve the system* — and @sec-solve-dont-invert explains,
in full, why.

# Why we are doing this: the oldest problem in the subject {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **when can a linear map be undone, and
what is the meaning of each way it can fail?** Every derivation below answers a piece of
that.

## The problem that predates "linear algebra"

Solving simultaneous linear equations is older than almost any other piece of mathematics
we still teach. The Chinese text *Jiuzhang Suanshu* (*The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical
Art*, compiled by roughly 200 BCE) devotes its eighth chapter, *Fangcheng* ("rectangular
arrays"), to systems like "three bundles of good grain, two of mediocre, one of poor, yield
39 *dou*; ..." — and solves them by laying the coefficients out in a rectangular array of
counting rods and eliminating one unknown at a time. That elimination procedure is, line
for line, the algorithm we now call **Gaussian elimination**, named for Gauss's
nineteenth-century use of it in least-squares astronomy but two millennia older than him.

The reason the problem is foundational is that *almost everything linear reduces to it*.
Fitting a regression line, balancing a chemical reaction, finding the currents in a circuit
(Kirchhoff's laws are linear), solving the discretized version of a differential equation,
computing the stationary distribution of a Markov chain — each is, at its core, "find the
$\mathbf{x}$ with $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$." The matrix $A$ encodes how the unknowns
combine; the vector $\mathbf{b}$ encodes what they must add up to. So the question "when can
I solve it, and when can't I" is the question of when these problems have answers at all.

## What "linear algebra" added to "solve the equations"

The old methods *computed* a solution when one existed but were silent about *why* it
existed, or what to do when it did not. Linear algebra's contribution — the contribution
this whole track is built on — was to stop treating $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ as a tangle of
scalar equations and start reading it as **a statement about vectors in space**. From
[GEOM-08] we have the reading that turns the lock:

$$
A\mathbf{x}
= \begin{bmatrix} | & | & & | \\ \mathbf{a}_1 & \mathbf{a}_2 & \cdots & \mathbf{a}_n \\ | & | & & | \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{bmatrix}
= x_1\mathbf{a}_1 + x_2\mathbf{a}_2 + \cdots + x_n\mathbf{a}_n,
$$ {#eq-columns}

where $\mathbf{a}_j$ is the $j$-th **column** of $A$ and $x_j$ is the $j$-th entry of
$\mathbf{x}$. (We re-derive this in @sec-columns-reading because it is the load-bearing
idea of the article.) Read this way, $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ no longer asks "what numbers
satisfy these equations" but "**with what weights $x_j$ do I combine the column-arrows
$\mathbf{a}_j$ to land on $\mathbf{b}$?**" Once you see the question in that form, all three
possible answers — one, none, or infinitely many — become statements about *where
$\mathbf{b}$ sits relative to the column-arrows*, which we can draw. That is the whole
article.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ such a big deal — isn't it just simultaneous equations
from high school?"* It is, and that is exactly the point: an enormous fraction of applied
mathematics is *secretly* a linear system, and the language of linear algebra lets you
answer the existence-and-uniqueness question **without solving** — by inspecting the
geometry of $A$'s columns. High-school algebra can grind out a solution when one exists;
linear algebra tells you *in advance* whether one exists, whether it is unique, and what to
do when it is not. That predictive power is what you are buying.
:::

# Intuition before symbols: three pictures {#sec-intuition}

Before any elimination, look at the three outcomes as pictures in the plane, where
$A$ is $2\times 2$ and its two columns $\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2$ are arrows.

The target $\mathbf{b}$ is some point. We are asked to reach it by walking $x_1$ steps
along $\mathbf{a}_1$ and $x_2$ steps along $\mathbf{a}_2$. Three things can happen.

1. **The two columns point in genuinely different directions.** Then they span the whole
   plane: *any* $\mathbf{b}$ is reachable, and reachable in exactly one way (one choice of
   $x_1$ and $x_2$). Unique solution. This is the invertible case.
2. **The two columns are parallel** (one is a scalar multiple of the other). Then every
   combination $x_1\mathbf{a}_1+x_2\mathbf{a}_2$ lands on the *single line* through both.
   If $\mathbf{b}$ is off that line, you can never reach it — **no solution**. If
   $\mathbf{b}$ happens to lie *on* that line, you can reach it, but in infinitely many ways
   (trade steps along one column for steps along the parallel other) — **infinitely many
   solutions**.

@fig-three-cases draws all three.

```{python}
#| label: fig-three-cases
#| fig-cap: 'The three outcomes of $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ in the plane, read as ''combine the column-arrows to reach $\mathbf{b}$.'' **Left:** independent columns span the plane — every target is reached exactly one way (invertible). **Center:** parallel columns span only a line; a target off that line (red) is unreachable — no solution. **Right:** parallel columns and a target *on* their line — reachable, but in infinitely many ways (two example combinations shown), so the solution is not unique.'
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.6, 3.5))

# --- Left: independent columns, unique solution ---
ax = axes[0]
a1 = np.array([2.0, 0.5]); a2 = np.array([0.5, 2.0])
b  = np.array([2.5, 2.5])
# solve for the weights
x = np.linalg.solve(np.column_stack([a1, a2]), b)
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a1, color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", label_offset=(0.25, -0.2))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a2, color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_2$", label_offset=(-0.35, 0.2))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), b, color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{b}$", label_offset=(0.3, 0.2))
# show the parallelogram path
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), x[0]*a1, color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--")
draw_vec(ax, x[0]*a1, b, color=GREY, lw=1.4, ls="--")
ax.set_title("Independent columns\n(unique solution)", fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 3.5); ax.set_aspect("equal")

# --- Center: parallel columns, b off the line, no solution ---
ax = axes[1]
a1 = np.array([2.0, 1.0]); a2 = np.array([1.0, 0.5])  # a2 = 0.5 a1
b  = np.array([1.0, 2.2])
ts = np.linspace(-1.5, 2.0, 50)
line = np.outer(ts, a1)
ax.plot(line[:, 0], line[:, 1], color=PALE, lw=6, alpha=0.7, zorder=0,
        label="reachable line")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a1, color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", label_offset=(0.25, -0.25))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a2, color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_2$", label_offset=(0.3, -0.25))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), b, color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{b}$", label_offset=(0.3, 0.2))
ax.set_title("Parallel columns,\n$\\mathbf{b}$ off the line (no solution)", fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(-1.0, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 3.0); ax.set_aspect("equal")

# --- Right: parallel columns, b on the line, infinitely many ---
ax = axes[2]
a1 = np.array([2.0, 1.0]); a2 = np.array([1.0, 0.5])
b  = np.array([3.0, 1.5])   # = 1.5 * a1 (on the line)
ts = np.linspace(-1.0, 2.2, 50)
line = np.outer(ts, a1)
ax.plot(line[:, 0], line[:, 1], color=PALE, lw=6, alpha=0.7, zorder=0,
        label="reachable line")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), a1, color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", label_offset=(0.25, -0.25))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), b, color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{b}$", label_offset=(0.3, 0.25))
ax.set_title("Parallel columns,\n$\\mathbf{b}$ on the line ($\\infty$ solutions)", fontsize=10)
ax.set_xlim(-1.0, 3.7); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 2.5); ax.set_aspect("equal")

plt.tight_layout()
```

The entire formal development is the precise, $n$-dimensional version of this picture. The
words we will make exact — *span*, *independent*, *column space*, *null space* — are just
the bookkeeping that lets us say which case we are in when we cannot draw the arrows.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The column reading, made precise {#sec-columns-reading}

We are given a matrix $A$ with $m$ rows and $n$ columns (an $m\times n$ matrix), an unknown
vector $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$, and a target $\mathbf{b}\in\mathbb{R}^m$. The product
$A\mathbf{x}$ was defined in [GEOM-08] in two equivalent ways; we need the
*column* reading and we re-derive it here so nothing is taken on faith.

Write $A$ by its columns, $A=[\mathbf{a}_1\ \mathbf{a}_2\ \cdots\ \mathbf{a}_n]$, where
each $\mathbf{a}_j\in\mathbb{R}^m$. By the definition of the matrix–vector product, the
$i$-th entry of $A\mathbf{x}$ is the dot product of the $i$-th *row* of $A$ with
$\mathbf{x}$:

$$
(A\mathbf{x})_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} A_{ij}\,x_j .
$$

Now read that sum the other way — group by $j$ instead of evaluating per $i$. The number
$A_{ij}$ is the $i$-th entry of the $j$-th column $\mathbf{a}_j$. So the whole vector
$A\mathbf{x}$, stacked over all rows $i$, is

$$
A\mathbf{x}
= \begin{bmatrix} \sum_j A_{1j}x_j \\ \sum_j A_{2j}x_j \\ \vdots \\ \sum_j A_{mj}x_j \end{bmatrix}
= \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j \begin{bmatrix} A_{1j} \\ A_{2j} \\ \vdots \\ A_{mj} \end{bmatrix}
= \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j\,\mathbf{a}_j ,
$$

which is exactly @eq-columns. The middle step is just the distributive law: a sum over $i$
*and* $j$ can be regrouped to sum the $j$-th column scaled by $x_j$. So:

::: {#def-system}
**Definition (the system, read geometrically).** Solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ for
$\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is *exactly* the problem of finding weights $x_1,\dots,x_n$ such
that

$$
x_1\mathbf{a}_1 + x_2\mathbf{a}_2 + \cdots + x_n\mathbf{a}_n = \mathbf{b}.
$$
:::

This recasting is the single most useful sentence in the article. It turns "solve the
equations" into "is $\mathbf{b}$ a linear combination of the columns, and if so with what
weights." The set of *all* linear combinations of the columns is, by definition from
[GEOM-02], the **column space** of $A$:

$$
\operatorname{Col}(A) = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf{a}_1,\dots,\mathbf{a}_n\}
= \{A\mathbf{x} : \mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^m .
$$ {#eq-colspace}

The second equality is again @eq-columns: as $\mathbf{x}$ ranges over all of
$\mathbb{R}^n$, $A\mathbf{x}$ ranges over all combinations of the columns. We can now state
the existence question in one line.

::: {#thm-existence}
**Theorem (existence).** The system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has *at least one* solution if
and only if $\mathbf{b}\in\operatorname{Col}(A)$ — that is, if and only if $\mathbf{b}$ can
be written as a combination of the columns of $A$.
:::

::: {.proof}
This is immediate from @def-system, but we spell out both directions. **($\Rightarrow$)** If
some $\mathbf{x}$ solves the system, then $\mathbf{b}=A\mathbf{x}=\sum_j x_j\mathbf{a}_j$ by
@eq-columns, which is a combination of the columns, so $\mathbf{b}\in\operatorname{Col}(A)$.
**($\Leftarrow$)** If $\mathbf{b}\in\operatorname{Col}(A)$, then by the definition of the
column space @eq-colspace there exist scalars $x_1,\dots,x_n$ with
$\mathbf{b}=\sum_j x_j\mathbf{a}_j$; collecting them into the vector
$\mathbf{x}=(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ gives $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ by @eq-columns. So a solution
exists. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The *no-solution* case of the Core is precisely the failure of @thm-existence:
$\mathbf{b}\notin\operatorname{Col}(A)$. We come back to it in @sec-no-solution. First we
handle the cleanest case — when there is exactly one solution and the map can be inverted.

## Gaussian elimination is a sequence of linear maps {#sec-elimination}

To *find* the solution (when it exists) we use elimination, the algorithm from the *Nine
Chapters*. The geometric insight that makes it modern — and that builds straight to the
inverse — is that **each elimination step is itself a linear map**, i.e. left-multiplication
by a matrix. We will use this to manufacture $A^{-1}$ for free.

Work with a square $A$ ($m=n$) for now; the rectangular case is @sec-rectangular. The three
operations elimination performs on the rows of $A$ (and simultaneously on $\mathbf{b}$) are:

1. **Add a multiple of one row to another.** Replace row $i$ by (row $i$) $+\ c\cdot$(row
   $k$).
2. **Scale a row** by a nonzero constant.
3. **Swap two rows.**

Each of these can be written as left-multiplication by an **elementary matrix** $E$, namely
the identity with that one operation applied to it. We show this for operation 1; the others
are identical in spirit. Let $E$ be the identity matrix $I$ with an extra $c$ placed in row
$i$, column $k$ (so $E_{ik}=c$, off the diagonal). Then for any matrix $M$, the product $EM$
has the same rows as $M$ except that row $i$ becomes

$$
(EM)_{i\cdot} = \sum_{p} E_{ip}\,M_{p\cdot}
= \underbrace{1\cdot M_{i\cdot}}_{\text{from } E_{ii}=1}
  + \underbrace{c\cdot M_{k\cdot}}_{\text{from } E_{ik}=c}
= M_{i\cdot} + c\,M_{k\cdot},
$$

which is exactly operation 1. Here $M_{i\cdot}$ denotes the $i$-th row of $M$, and the sum
collapses because $E$'s $i$-th row has only two nonzero entries: the $1$ on the diagonal and
the $c$ in column $k$. So performing an elimination step on the system is the same as
multiplying both sides on the left by an elementary matrix $E$:

$$
A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
EA\mathbf{x}=E\mathbf{b}.
$$

This does not change the solution set: $E$ is invertible (we can undo "add $c$ times row
$k$ to row $i$" by "subtract $c$ times row $k$ from row $i$," another elementary matrix), so
$EA\mathbf{x}=E\mathbf{b}$ holds for the same $\mathbf{x}$ as $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$.
Elimination applies a sequence $E_1, E_2, \dots, E_K$ until the left side becomes the
identity:

$$
E_K\cdots E_2 E_1 A = I .
$$ {#eq-elim-to-I}

When elimination *succeeds* in reaching $I$ — when there is a pivot in every row and
column, no zero ever blocking the way — the matrix $A$ is invertible, and @eq-elim-to-I
hands us the inverse directly.

## The inverse, defined and computed {#sec-inverse}

::: {#def-inverse}
**Definition (inverse matrix).** A square matrix $A$ ($n\times n$) is **invertible** (or
**nonsingular**) if there is a matrix $A^{-1}$ of the same size with

$$
A^{-1}A = I \qquad\text{and}\qquad A A^{-1} = I,
$$ {#eq-inverse-def}

where $I$ is the $n\times n$ identity (ones on the diagonal, zeros elsewhere: the matrix of
the map that leaves every vector fixed, since $I\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x}$). If no such matrix
exists, $A$ is **singular** (non-invertible).
:::

The meaning is the whole point: $A$ takes $\mathbf{x}$ to $A\mathbf{x}$; $A^{-1}$ takes it
back. The map $A^{-1}$ *undoes* $A$. That is why @eq-inverse-def reads "do $A$ then $A^{-1}$
and you are back where you started" ($A^{-1}A=I$), and "do $A^{-1}$ then $A$ and likewise"
($AA^{-1}=I$). Once $A^{-1}$ exists, the system solves itself: left-multiply
$A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ by $A^{-1}$,

$$
A^{-1}(A\mathbf{x}) = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(A^{-1}A)\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
I\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\boxed{\;\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}.\;}
$$ {#eq-solve}

The middle steps use only associativity of matrix multiplication (proved in [GEOM-09]) and
the definition $A^{-1}A=I$, $I\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{x}$. And the solution is **unique**: if
$\mathbf{x}_1$ and $\mathbf{x}_2$ both solved the system, then
$A\mathbf{x}_1=A\mathbf{x}_2=\mathbf{b}$, so $A(\mathbf{x}_1-\mathbf{x}_2)=\mathbf{0}$;
left-multiplying by $A^{-1}$ gives $\mathbf{x}_1-\mathbf{x}_2 = A^{-1}\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}$,
hence $\mathbf{x}_1=\mathbf{x}_2$.

**Where does $A^{-1}$ come from?** From @eq-elim-to-I. Set $B = E_K\cdots E_2 E_1$, the
product of all the elimination steps. Then @eq-elim-to-I says $BA=I$. For square matrices
this one-sided identity already forces $B=A^{-1}$ — we prove that in @lem-onesided below —
so $A^{-1} = E_K\cdots E_2 E_1$. This is the recipe behind the standard **Gauss–Jordan**
computation: write $A$ and $I$ side by side as $[\,A \mid I\,]$, run elimination until the
left block becomes $I$, and whatever the right block became is $A^{-1}$ — because the same
product $B$ that turned $A$ into $I$ turned $I$ into $BI=B=A^{-1}$.

::: {#lem-onesided}
**Lemma (a one-sided inverse of a square matrix is the inverse).** If $A$ and $B$ are
$n\times n$ and $BA=I$, then also $AB=I$, so $B=A^{-1}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
First, $BA=I$ forces the columns of $A$ to be linearly independent: if $A\mathbf{v}
=\mathbf{0}$ then $\mathbf{v}=I\mathbf{v}=(BA)\mathbf{v}=B(A\mathbf{v})=B\mathbf{0}
=\mathbf{0}$, so the only vector $A$ sends to $\mathbf{0}$ is $\mathbf{0}$ itself. By
@thm-uniqueness (next section) that independence means every system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{c}$
has *at most* one solution; and because $A$ is $n\times n$ with $n$ independent columns,
those columns are a basis of $\mathbb{R}^n$ (a set of $n$ independent vectors in
$\mathbb{R}^n$ spans it — the basis fact from [GEOM-03]), so every $\mathbf{c}$ is reachable
and the solution *exists*. Thus $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{c}$ has a unique solution for every
$\mathbf{c}$; collecting the solutions of $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{e}_j$ for the standard basis
vectors $\mathbf{e}_j$ into the columns of a matrix $C$ gives $AC=I$. Now both $BA=I$ and
$AC=I$ hold, and associativity gives $B = B(AC) = (BA)C = C$, so $AB=AC=I$. Hence $B$ is a
genuine two-sided inverse. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does a matrix ever have a left inverse but not a right inverse?"* For a **square** matrix,
no — @lem-onesided shows a one-sided inverse is automatically two-sided. For a **rectangular**
matrix it absolutely can: a tall $m\times n$ matrix ($m>n$) with independent columns has a
left inverse $(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ but no right inverse (it cannot be onto a bigger space).
That left inverse *is* the least-squares solver — it reappears in @sec-rectangular and is
the heart of Regression R5. Knowing that the square one-sided result does **not** extend to
rectangular matrices is exactly the kind of edge an interviewer probes.
:::

## Uniqueness and the null space {#sec-uniqueness}

Existence (@thm-existence) is about $\mathbf{b}$ reaching the column space. *Uniqueness* is
a separate question, and it is about $A$ alone, not $\mathbf{b}$. The object that governs it
is the **null space**.

::: {#def-nullspace}
**Definition (null space).** The **null space** (or kernel) of $A$ is the set of vectors the
map crushes to the origin:

$$
\operatorname{Null}(A) = \{\mathbf{v}\in\mathbb{R}^n : A\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}\}.
$$
:::

The null space always contains $\mathbf{0}$ (since $A\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}$). The question is
whether it contains *anything else*.

::: {#thm-uniqueness}
**Theorem (uniqueness).** Suppose $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has at least one solution
$\mathbf{x}_0$. Then the *complete* set of solutions is

$$
\{\mathbf{x}_0 + \mathbf{v} : \mathbf{v}\in\operatorname{Null}(A)\}.
$$

Consequently the solution is **unique if and only if $\operatorname{Null}(A)=\{\mathbf{0}\}$**
— i.e. the only vector $A$ sends to zero is zero itself, equivalently the columns of $A$ are
linearly independent.
:::

::: {.proof}
Let $\mathbf{x}_0$ be one solution, so $A\mathbf{x}_0=\mathbf{b}$. Take any other vector
$\mathbf{x}$ and write $\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{x}_0$. Then, using linearity of the map
($A(\mathbf{p}+\mathbf{q})=A\mathbf{p}+A\mathbf{q}$, from [GEOM-08]),

$$
A\mathbf{x} = A(\mathbf{x}_0+\mathbf{v}) = A\mathbf{x}_0 + A\mathbf{v}
= \mathbf{b} + A\mathbf{v}.
$$

So $\mathbf{x}$ is a solution ($A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$) *if and only if* $A\mathbf{v}
=\mathbf{0}$, i.e. $\mathbf{v}\in\operatorname{Null}(A)$. That is precisely the claimed
solution set. For the uniqueness statement: if $\operatorname{Null}(A)=\{\mathbf{0}\}$ the
only allowed $\mathbf{v}$ is $\mathbf{0}$, leaving the single solution $\mathbf{x}_0$;
conversely if $\operatorname{Null}(A)$ contains some $\mathbf{v}\neq\mathbf{0}$ then
$\mathbf{x}_0$ and $\mathbf{x}_0+\mathbf{v}$ are two distinct solutions. Finally, the
equivalence "$\operatorname{Null}(A)=\{\mathbf{0}\}$ iff the columns are independent" is
just @eq-columns read as a definition: $A\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$ means
$\sum_j v_j\mathbf{a}_j=\mathbf{0}$, and the only such combination being the trivial one
($\mathbf{v}=\mathbf{0}$) is the *definition* of linear independence of the columns
([GEOM-03]). $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This theorem is the engine behind the "infinitely many" branch of the Core. If the columns
are dependent — one is a redundant combination of the others — then $\operatorname{Null}(A)$
is more than just the origin: it contains a whole line (or plane, or higher) of directions
the map collapses. For any reachable $\mathbf{b}$, you can take a solution and slide it
along that direction and still solve the system, because $A$ does not see the slide. **The
solutions form a copy of the null space, shifted to pass through any one solution.**
@fig-solution-set shows it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-solution-set
#| fig-cap: 'When the columns are dependent the solution set is a *line*, not a point. Here $A$ has a one-dimensional null space (the dashed direction $\mathbf{v}$, which $A$ crushes to $\mathbf{0}$). Any particular solution $\mathbf{x}_0$ plus any multiple of $\mathbf{v}$ is still a solution: the full solution set is the green line $\mathbf{x}_0 + t\,\mathbf{v}$. Uniqueness fails precisely because the null space is bigger than the single point $\mathbf{0}$.'
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 4.6))
x0 = np.array([1.0, 0.5])
v  = np.array([1.0, 1.4])   # null-space direction
ts = np.linspace(-1.6, 1.8, 50)
sol = x0[:, None] + np.outer(v, ts)
ax.plot(sol[0], sol[1], color=ACCENT, lw=3, label=r"solution set $\mathbf{x}_0 + t\,\mathbf{v}$")
ax.scatter(*x0, color=HILITE, s=70, zorder=5, label=r"a particular solution $\mathbf{x}_0$")
# draw the null-space direction as an arrow at origin
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v, color=GREY, lw=2.0, ls="--", label=r"$\mathbf{v}\in\mathrm{Null}(A)$",
         label_offset=(0.3, 0.15))
ax.scatter(0, 0, color="black", s=30, zorder=5)
ax.text(0.05, -0.28, r"$\mathbf{0}$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_xlim(-1.5, 3.0); ax.set_ylim(-2.0, 3.0); ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_title("Dependent columns: infinitely many solutions")
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The full verdict: one theorem ties existence and uniqueness together {#sec-verdict}

For a **square** $A$ the two questions collapse into one, and this is the cleanest result of
the article.

::: {#thm-invertible}
**Theorem (invertibility = full rank, square case).** For an $n\times n$ matrix $A$, the
following are equivalent:

1. $A$ is invertible (an $A^{-1}$ exists).
2. The columns of $A$ are linearly independent.
3. The columns of $A$ span all of $\mathbb{R}^n$ (so $\operatorname{Col}(A)=\mathbb{R}^n$).
4. $\operatorname{Null}(A)=\{\mathbf{0}\}$.
5. $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has a *unique* solution for **every** $\mathbf{b}$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We show the equivalences by chaining what we already have. **(2)$\Leftrightarrow$(4)** is
the last line of @thm-uniqueness. **(2)$\Leftrightarrow$(3):** $n$ vectors in
$\mathbb{R}^n$ are independent if and only if they form a basis, i.e. if and only if they
also span $\mathbb{R}^n$ — this is the basis-counting fact from [GEOM-03] (in a space of
dimension $n$, any $n$ independent vectors span, and any $n$ spanning vectors are
independent). **(3)$\Rightarrow$ existence for every $\mathbf{b}$:** if the columns span
$\mathbb{R}^n$ then every $\mathbf{b}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ lies in $\operatorname{Col}(A)$, so by
@thm-existence a solution exists. Combined with **(4)** (uniqueness), this gives **(5)**:
existence-and-uniqueness for every $\mathbf{b}$. **(5)$\Rightarrow$(1):** if every
$A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{e}_j$ has a unique solution $\mathbf{c}_j$ (for the standard basis
vectors $\mathbf{e}_1,\dots,\mathbf{e}_n$), collect the $\mathbf{c}_j$ as the columns of a
matrix $C$; then $AC=I$ by construction, and by @lem-onesided this $C$ is a genuine
two-sided inverse, so $A$ is invertible. **(1)$\Rightarrow$(5):** shown already at
@eq-solve (existence $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ and the uniqueness argument right after
it). The cycle closes, so all five are equivalent. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This is why the Core could say it in one breath: *the inverse exists if and only if the
columns are a basis.* Independent (no redundancy, condition 2) **and** spanning (reach
everything, condition 3) are the two halves of "basis," and for a square matrix each implies
the other. We will meet two more equivalent conditions later in the track and only **flag**
them here, because each is built properly in its own article:

- **"$A$ has full rank $n$"** — the rank is the dimension of the column space ([GEOM-12]),
  and full rank $n$ for an $n\times n$ matrix is exactly condition 3.
- **"$\det A \neq 0$"** — the determinant is the signed volume of the image of the unit cube
  ([GEOM-13]); it is zero precisely when the map collapses space, which is exactly the
  failure of conditions 2–3.

These are genuinely the *same* condition wearing three costumes (rank, determinant, the
basis property). We are not deferring the *explanation* — @thm-invertible is complete on its
own — we are only naming the two equivalent diagnostics that other articles build.

## The algebra of inverses: two identities, proved {#sec-inverse-algebra}

The inverse interacts with products and transposes in ways we will use constantly
downstream (the normal equations of Regression are built from exactly these moves). We prove
both from the definition @eq-inverse-def — nothing is asserted.

::: {#thm-inverse-product}
**Theorem (inverse of a product).** If $A$ and $B$ are invertible $n\times n$ matrices, then
$AB$ is invertible and

$$
(AB)^{-1} = B^{-1}A^{-1}.
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
To show a matrix $M$ is the inverse of $AB$, we need only check $M(AB)=I$ (then @lem-onesided
gives the other side automatically). Take $M=B^{-1}A^{-1}$ and compute, using associativity
([GEOM-09]) to regroup and the definitions $A^{-1}A=I$, $B^{-1}B=I$, $IB=B$:

$$
(B^{-1}A^{-1})(AB)
= B^{-1}(A^{-1}A)B
= B^{-1} I B
= B^{-1}B
= I.
$$

So $B^{-1}A^{-1}$ is the inverse of $AB$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The order-reversal is not an algebraic accident; it is the geometry of "undo." If you put on
socks ($B$) then shoes ($A$), undoing means taking off shoes first ($A^{-1}$) then socks
($B^{-1}$): $(AB)^{-1}=B^{-1}A^{-1}$. You undo composed maps in reverse order — exactly the
order-reversal that the transpose obeys in [GEOM-10], for the same structural reason.

::: {#thm-inverse-transpose}
**Theorem (inverse of a transpose).** If $A$ is invertible, so is $A^\top$, and

$$
(A^\top)^{-1} = (A^{-1})^\top .
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
We use the transpose-of-a-product identity $(MN)^\top = N^\top M^\top$ proved in [GEOM-10],
and the fact that $I^\top = I$ (the identity is symmetric — flipping it across the diagonal
changes nothing). Start from the defining relation $A^{-1}A=I$ and transpose both sides:

$$
(A^{-1}A)^\top = I^\top
\;\Longrightarrow\;
A^\top (A^{-1})^\top = I .
$$

This says $(A^{-1})^\top$ is a *right* inverse of $A^\top$. Doing the same to $AA^{-1}=I$
gives $(A^{-1})^\top A^\top = I$, a left inverse. Having both, $(A^{-1})^\top$ is the inverse
of $A^\top$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The notation $A^{-\top}$ is common shorthand for this single object $(A^\top)^{-1}=(A^{-1})^\top$.
The two operations — invert and transpose — commute, so the order you apply them in does not
matter.

# The no-solution case: the bridge back to projection {#sec-no-solution}

We have handled "exactly one" (invertible) and "infinitely many" (nontrivial null space).
The third outcome — **no solution** — is the one that, far from being a dead end, opens the
door to the most important construction in the whole track. It is worth the most care.

By @thm-existence, no solution means $\mathbf{b}\notin\operatorname{Col}(A)$: the target is
not reachable by any combination of the columns. This is the *normal* situation in data
science, not a pathology. Whenever you have **more equations than unknowns** — more data
points than parameters, the **over-determined** case ($m>n$, a tall thin $A$) — the column
space is a low-dimensional slice of a high-dimensional $\mathbb{R}^m$, and a noisy
$\mathbf{b}$ will almost surely miss it. Three measurements of a quantity that should agree
never quite do; the system "all three equal the true value" has no exact solution. That is
the over-determined problem that *birthed* least squares, the same one Legendre and Gauss
faced (and the opening of the Regression exemplar, REGR-01).

So what do we do when we cannot hit $\mathbf{b}$? **We hit the closest point we can.** We
replace the unsolvable $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ with the solvable

$$
A\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{b}^\star, \qquad
\mathbf{b}^\star = \text{the point of } \operatorname{Col}(A) \text{ closest to } \mathbf{b},
$$

and accept the unavoidable gap $\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{b}^\star$. That closest point
$\mathbf{b}^\star$ is the **orthogonal projection** of $\mathbf{b}$ onto the column space,
the keystone construction of [GEOM-06]. @fig-no-solution draws the geometry in
$\mathbb{R}^3$: a two-dimensional column-space plane, a target $\mathbf{b}$ floating off it,
and the perpendicular dropped to the nearest reachable point.

```{python}
#| label: fig-no-solution
#| fig-cap: 'The no-solution case, and its repair. The columns of a tall $A$ span a plane (the column space) inside $\mathbb{R}^3$. The target $\mathbf{b}$ (red) sits *off* the plane, so $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has no solution. The honest response is to solve $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}=\mathbf{b}^\star$ where $\mathbf{b}^\star$ (green) is the closest point of the plane to $\mathbf{b}$ — its orthogonal projection. The leftover $\mathbf{b}-\mathbf{b}^\star$ (dashed) is perpendicular to the plane: it is the residual that least squares minimizes.'
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.6, 5.2))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")

# two columns spanning a plane through the origin
a1 = np.array([1.0, 0.2, 0.0])
a2 = np.array([0.3, 1.0, 0.0])
# the plane (rotate it a bit so it isn't axis-aligned)
R = np.array([[1, 0, 0],
              [0, np.cos(0.5), -np.sin(0.5)],
              [0, np.sin(0.5),  np.cos(0.5)]])
a1 = R @ a1; a2 = R @ a2
ss = np.linspace(-1.4, 1.8, 12); tt = np.linspace(-1.4, 1.8, 12)
S, T = np.meshgrid(ss, tt)
P = (S[..., None] * a1 + T[..., None] * a2)
ax.plot_surface(P[..., 0], P[..., 1], P[..., 2], alpha=0.30, color=PALE,
                linewidth=0, zorder=0)

# target b off the plane
b = np.array([1.2, 0.7, 1.3])
# projection of b onto span(a1, a2): solve normal equations for the picture
Acol = np.column_stack([a1, a2])
xhat = np.linalg.solve(Acol.T @ Acol, Acol.T @ b)
bstar = Acol @ xhat

ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, *a1, color=ACCENT, arrow_length_ratio=0.12, lw=2)
ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, *a2, color=ACCENT, arrow_length_ratio=0.12, lw=2)
ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, *b, color=HILITE, arrow_length_ratio=0.10, lw=2.5)
ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, *bstar, color=ACCENT, arrow_length_ratio=0.12, lw=2.5)
ax.plot([b[0], bstar[0]], [b[1], bstar[1]], [b[2], bstar[2]],
        color=GREY, ls="--", lw=2)

ax.text(*(a1 * 1.15), r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", color=ACCENT)
ax.text(*(a2 * 1.15), r"$\mathbf{a}_2$", color=ACCENT)
ax.text(*(b * 1.08), r"$\mathbf{b}$", color=HILITE)
ax.text(*(bstar * 1.10 + np.array([0, 0, -0.15])), r"$\mathbf{b}^\star$", color=ACCENT)
ax.text(*((b + bstar) / 2 + np.array([0.15, 0, 0.1])), "residual", color=GREY, fontsize=9)
ax.set_title("No solution: project $\\mathbf{b}$ onto the column space")
ax.set_xlabel("x"); ax.set_ylabel("y"); ax.set_zlabel("z")
ax.view_init(elev=22, azim=-60)
ax.set_box_aspect((1, 1, 0.8))
plt.tight_layout()
```

The condition that pins down $\mathbf{b}^\star$ is that the residual $\mathbf{b}-A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$
must be **orthogonal to every column** of $A$ — "perpendicular to the plane." Writing
"orthogonal to every column" as a single matrix equation (the columns of $A$ dotted with the
residual all vanish, i.e. $A^\top(\mathbf{b}-A\hat{\mathbf{x}})=\mathbf{0}$) gives the
**normal equations**

$$
A^\top A\,\hat{\mathbf{x}} = A^\top \mathbf{b},
$$ {#eq-normal-eq}

and, *when $A^\top A$ is invertible*, the least-squares solution

$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^\top A)^{-1} A^\top \mathbf{b}.
$$ {#eq-lsq}

We are not deriving the projection here — that derivation, including *why* orthogonality of
the residual is equivalent to closeness, is the entire content of [GEOM-06], and we honor the
track's ownership by pointing there rather than re-running it. What this article contributes
is the *connection*: the unsolvable system has a best-possible stand-in, and computing that
stand-in is itself a solvable square system @eq-normal-eq in the smaller matrix $A^\top A$.
The object $(A^\top A)^{-1}$ in @eq-lsq is exactly the $(X^\top X)^{-1}$ that Regression R5
revolves around; everything we proved about inverses applies to it, and the condition for it
to exist ($A$ has independent columns, so $A^\top A$ is invertible — proved in [GEOM-18] via
positive-definiteness) is the very full-rank condition of @thm-invertible applied to the
slimmer matrix.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has no solution, what does $A^{-1}$ even mean — and what does
your software return?"* If $A$ is rectangular (the usual over-determined case), $A^{-1}$ does
not exist at all — only square matrices can have inverses, because @eq-inverse-def needs
$A^{-1}A$ and $AA^{-1}$ to both be the *same-size* identity. What the least-squares machinery
returns in @eq-lsq is the **left inverse** $(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$ (a wide $n\times m$
matrix). It satisfies $\big[(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top\big]A = I_n$ but *not* the other order. The
fully general object that works for *any* shape and *any* rank — square or not, full rank or
not — is the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** $A^{+}$, built from the SVD in [GEOM-21]; for a
tall full-rank $A$ it reduces to exactly $(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top$, and `numpy.linalg.pinv`
computes it. Saying "$A$ has no inverse, but it has a pseudoinverse that gives the
least-squares/minimum-norm solution" is the answer that shows you understand all three cases
at once.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run all three outcomes on honest numbers, and include an example at the **edge of
applicability** — a system that is technically invertible but numerically a trap, which
motivates the practitioner's rule in @sec-solve-dont-invert.

## Example 1 — a well-posed $3\times 3$ system: mixing a fertilizer blend {#sec-ex-fertilizer}

A genuinely linear, genuinely invertible problem. A grower has three stock fertilizers and
needs a blend hitting a target nutrient profile. Stock 1 is $(N,P,K)=(10,5,5)$ percent by
weight; stock 2 is $(4,10,6)$; stock 3 is $(6,4,12)$. Mixing $x_1, x_2, x_3$ kilograms of
each delivers nutrients $x_1\mathbf{a}_1 + x_2\mathbf{a}_2 + x_3\mathbf{a}_3$ — a linear
combination of the columns, *exactly* @def-system. The target is $\mathbf{b}=(86,77,94)$ (in
the same percent-kilograms units). We solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-fertilizer
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
A = np.array([[10.0, 4.0, 6.0],    # Nitrogen row
              [ 5.0, 10.0, 4.0],   # Phosphorus row
              [ 5.0, 6.0, 12.0]])  # Potassium row
b = np.array([86.0, 77.0, 94.0])

# The recommended way: SOLVE, do not form the inverse (see section on this).
x = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
print("solution x (kg of each stock):", np.round(x, 4))

# Verify by reconstruction: A x should reproduce b.
print("A @ x reproduces b? ", np.allclose(A @ x, b), "  ->", np.round(A @ x, 3))

# For illustration only, the inverse exists and gives the same answer:
Ainv = np.linalg.inv(A)
print("same x via A^{-1} b:", np.round(Ainv @ b, 4))
print("A^{-1} A = I? ", np.allclose(Ainv @ A, np.eye(3)))
```

The columns point in three genuinely different nutrient directions, so they span
$\mathbb{R}^3$: every reachable profile is reachable in exactly one way (@thm-invertible).
The solver returns the unique blend, and reconstruction confirms it. The code also forms
$A^{-1}$ *for illustration* and recovers the same $\mathbf{x}$ — but note we led with
`solve`, not `inv`, for the reason developed in @sec-solve-dont-invert. The verdict here is
the clean one: independent columns, trivial null space, unique solution.

## Example 2 — no solution: an over-determined sensor reconciliation {#sec-ex-overdetermined}

Now the data-science default. Four temperature sensors are placed along a thin metal rod and
report readings; physics says the temperature should be **linear in position**,
$T(p)=c_0 + c_1 p$, so the model has only $n=2$ unknowns $(c_0,c_1)$ but $m=4$ noisy
equations. The design matrix $A$ stacks $[1, p_i]$ rows; $\mathbf{b}$ is the four readings.
With noise, no straight line passes through all four points: $\mathbf{b}$ lies *off* the
two-dimensional column space, and $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ has **no exact solution**.

```{python}
#| label: fig-overdetermined
#| fig-cap: 'An over-determined system ($m=4$ equations, $n=2$ unknowns) with no exact solution. **Left:** four noisy sensor readings; no line hits all four, so $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ is unsolvable. The least-squares line (red) solves the normal equations $A^\top A\hat{\mathbf{x}}=A^\top\mathbf{b}$ instead — the best reachable stand-in. **Right:** the residuals $\mathbf{b}-A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$, the unavoidable gap between the target and the column space. They sum to zero and are uncorrelated with position — the orthogonality condition of the normal equations, made visible.'
pos = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
read = np.array([20.1, 22.4, 23.3, 25.9])     # noisy, not collinear
A = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(pos), pos])  # 4 x 2 design matrix
b = read

# No exact solution: confirm b is not reachable.
# Least-squares stand-in via the normal equations (square 2x2 solve).
xhat = np.linalg.solve(A.T @ A, A.T @ b)
fitted = A @ xhat
resid = b - fitted
ps = np.linspace(-0.3, 3.3, 50)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.7))
axL.scatter(pos, read, color=VEC, s=55, zorder=3, label="sensor readings $\\mathbf{b}$")
axL.plot(ps, xhat[0] + xhat[1] * ps, color=HILITE,
         label=f"LSQ line $T={xhat[0]:.2f}+{xhat[1]:.2f}p$")
for p, r, f in zip(pos, read, fitted):
    axL.plot([p, p], [r, f], color=GREY, lw=1.4)
axL.set_xlabel("position $p$"); axL.set_ylabel("temperature $T$")
axL.set_title("No exact solution -> best reachable line")
axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(pos, resid, color=VEC, s=55, zorder=3)
for p, r in zip(pos, resid):
    axR.plot([p, p], [0, r], color=GREY, lw=1.4)
axR.set_xlabel("position $p$"); axR.set_ylabel("residual")
axR.set_title("Residual = gap to the column space")
plt.tight_layout()

print("normal-equation solution xhat (c0, c1):", np.round(xhat, 4))
print("sum of residuals            :", f"{resid.sum(): .3e}  (~0, orthogonal to the all-ones column)")
print("sum of position * residual  :", f"{np.dot(pos, resid): .3e}  (~0, orthogonal to the position column)")
```

The two printed checks are @eq-normal-eq made operational: the residual is orthogonal to
both columns of $A$ (the all-ones column and the position column), so the two dot products
vanish. There was no $\mathbf{x}$ with $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ — but there is a unique
$\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ making $A\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ the *closest* point of the column space to
$\mathbf{b}$. This is the no-solution branch of the Core, and it is the entire reason
least-squares regression exists. Reading it the geometry way: $\mathbf{b}\in\mathbb{R}^4$
could not be reached from a 2-dimensional plane, so we settled for the foot of the
perpendicular.

## Example 3 — infinitely many: an under-determined / rank-deficient system {#sec-ex-underdetermined}

The third branch. Suppose two of our "independent" predictors are secretly the same
information in different units — say a length recorded once in meters and once in feet, so
column 2 is exactly $3.281\times$ column 1. The columns are then **dependent**: the null
space is nontrivial, and any solvable target is solved in infinitely many ways.

```{python}
#| label: fig-underdetermined
#| fig-cap: 'A rank-deficient system: column 2 is a scalar multiple of column 1 (the same feature in different units). Its null space is the dashed line of weight-vectors $\mathbf{x}$ that $A$ crushes to $\mathbf{0}$ — trade weight off column 1 against column 2 in the fixed ratio and the prediction never changes. Every dot on the green line is an exact solution of $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$; the minimum-norm one (filled) is what the pseudoinverse returns.'
# A: 3 rows, 2 columns; column 2 = 3.281 * column 1  => rank 1, dependent.
col1 = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 0.5])
A = np.column_stack([col1, 3.281 * col1])      # 3 x 2, rank 1
# pick a b that IS reachable (in the column space) so solutions exist
x_true = np.array([2.0, -0.3])
b = A @ x_true

# Null space direction: A v = 0  <=>  v1 col1 + v2 (3.281 col1) = 0
# => v1 + 3.281 v2 = 0  => v = (3.281, -1) up to scale.
v = np.array([3.281, -1.0]); v = v / np.linalg.norm(v)

# minimum-norm solution via the pseudoinverse
x_min = np.linalg.pinv(A) @ b

ts = np.linspace(-3, 3, 50)
sols = x_min[:, None] + np.outer(v, ts)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.8, 4.8))
ax.plot(sols[0], sols[1], color=ACCENT, lw=3, label=r"all solutions $\mathbf{x}_0+t\mathbf{v}$")
ax.scatter(*x_true, color=VEC, s=55, zorder=5, label="one solution")
ax.scatter(*x_min, color=HILITE, s=80, zorder=6, label="minimum-norm solution")
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), v * 2, color=GREY, lw=1.8, ls="--",
         label=r"$\mathbf{v}\in\mathrm{Null}(A)$", label_offset=(0.35, 0.2))
ax.scatter(0, 0, color="black", s=25, zorder=5)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$ (weight on meters column)")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$ (weight on feet column)")
ax.set_title("Dependent columns: a whole line of solutions")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("rank of A           :", np.linalg.matrix_rank(A), " (out of 2 columns -> rank deficient)")
print("a particular x_true :", np.round(x_true, 3), "  -> A x =", np.round(A @ x_true, 3))
x_alt = x_true + 1.7 * v
print("a different solution:", np.round(x_alt, 3), "  -> A x =", np.round(A @ x_alt, 3), " (same b!)")
print("minimum-norm  x     :", np.round(x_min, 3))
```

Two different weight vectors produce the *identical* prediction $\mathbf{b}$, because they
differ by a null-space direction $A$ ignores. This is the geometry of @thm-uniqueness: the
solution set is the green line $\mathbf{x}_0 + t\mathbf{v}$. In a regression context this is
**perfect multicollinearity** — the model cannot decide how to split credit between two
identical predictors, so $(A^\top A)^{-1}$ does not even exist and the coefficients are not
identified. The honest fixes (drop the redundant column, or pick the unique minimum-norm
solution via the pseudoinverse, @sec-no-solution callout) live in [GEOM-21] and in
Regression R7; the *diagnosis* — a nontrivial null space — is what this article gives you.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

## The honest edge: invertible on paper, useless in practice {#sec-ill-conditioned}

A matrix can be perfectly invertible — full rank, nonzero determinant, every theorem in
@sec-verdict satisfied — and still give a *garbage* answer on a computer. This is the most
important practitioner caveat in the article, and it is the case at the edge of
applicability that the constitution demands. The trouble is **near**-singularity: columns
that are not parallel but *almost* parallel. The map then squashes one direction nearly (but
not quite) to zero, and inverting it *blows that direction back up* by a huge factor —
amplifying the tiny floating-point and measurement errors in $\mathbf{b}$ into enormous
errors in $\mathbf{x}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-illcond
#| fig-cap: 'An invertible-but-treacherous system. The two columns of $A$ are *almost* parallel (the column space is a thin sliver), so $A$ is technically invertible yet nearly singular. **Left:** the columns and two nearby targets $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{b}+\delta$ differing by a tiny perturbation. **Right:** their solutions $\mathbf{x}$ are wildly far apart — a minuscule change in $\mathbf{b}$ swings the answer enormously. This is large condition number $\kappa(A)$ (GEOM-26): the reason ''invertible'' is not the same as ''safe to invert.'''
# nearly-parallel columns
A = np.array([[1.0, 1.0],
              [1.0, 1.0 + 1e-3]])
b  = np.array([2.0, 2.0])
db = np.array([0.0, 1e-3])           # tiny perturbation of b
x  = np.linalg.solve(A, b)
x2 = np.linalg.solve(A, b + db)
kappa = np.linalg.cond(A)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9.2, 3.8))
# left: the two near-parallel columns
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), A[:, 0], color=ACCENT, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_1$", label_offset=(0.06, -0.12))
draw_vec(axL, (0, 0), A[:, 1], color=PURPLE, label=r"$\mathbf{a}_2$", label_offset=(0.06, 0.12))
axL.set_title(f"Almost-parallel columns\n$\\kappa(A)\\approx{kappa:.0f}$", fontsize=10)
axL.set_xlim(-0.2, 1.4); axL.set_ylim(-0.2, 1.4); axL.set_aspect("equal")

# right: the two solutions, far apart
axR.scatter(*x, color=VEC, s=70, zorder=4, label=r"$\mathbf{x}$ for $\mathbf{b}$")
axR.scatter(*x2, color=HILITE, s=70, zorder=4, label=r"$\mathbf{x}$ for $\mathbf{b}+\delta$")
axR.annotate("", xy=x2, xytext=x,
             arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=GREY, lw=1.6))
axR.set_title("Tiny change in $\\mathbf{b}$ -> huge change in $\\mathbf{x}$", fontsize=10)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$x_1$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$x_2$")
axR.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("solution for b        :", np.round(x, 4))
print("solution for b + delta:", np.round(x2, 4))
print(f"||delta b|| / ||b|| = {np.linalg.norm(db)/np.linalg.norm(b):.2e}   (input wiggle)")
print(f"||delta x|| / ||x|| = {np.linalg.norm(x2-x)/np.linalg.norm(x):.2e}   (output swing)")
print(f"condition number kappa(A) = {kappa:.1f}")
```

A relative wiggle of $10^{-3}$ in the input produces a relative swing of order $1$ in the
output — the error was amplified roughly $\kappa(A)$-fold, where $\kappa(A)$ is the
**condition number**. This is not a bug in the solver; it is intrinsic to the *problem*. The
condition number is the single number that quantifies it, and it gets its full geometric
derivation (as the ratio of the largest to smallest singular value) in [GEOM-26]. The lesson
for now: **"$A$ is invertible" is a yes/no fact; "the inverse can be trusted" is a matter of
degree**, governed by $\kappa$.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Never test singularity with $\det A \approx 0$, and never decide a system is "fine"
because $\det A \neq 0$.** The determinant scales with the *units* and *size* of the matrix:
a perfectly well-conditioned matrix can have a determinant of $10^{-30}$ (just scale a
clean matrix by $0.1$ in a $30$-dimensional space), and a near-singular matrix can have
$\det = 1$. The determinant answers the *binary* question "exactly singular?" but says
almost nothing about the *practical* question "safe to invert?". The right diagnostic is the
**condition number** $\kappa(A)$ ([GEOM-26]), not the determinant. We flag this here because
it is the single most common beginner mistake with linear systems.
:::

## Solve, don't invert {#sec-solve-dont-invert}

This is the practitioner's rule the whole article has been building toward, and now we can
justify it completely. Suppose you must solve $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$. You have two
options:

1. **Form $A^{-1}$, then multiply:** $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.
2. **Solve the system directly** (Gaussian elimination / LU factorization, or for special
   structure a Cholesky ([GEOM-19]) or QR solve), never naming $A^{-1}$.

Option 2 is faster, more accurate, and more memory-frugal — for reasons that follow directly
from what we have proved, not from any deep numerical mysticism:

- **Cost.** Solving one system by elimination costs about $\tfrac{1}{3}n^3$ arithmetic
  operations. *Forming* $A^{-1}$ amounts to solving $n$ systems (one per column of $I$, as in
  @lem-onesided) and costs about $n^3$ — three times as much — and then you *still* have to
  multiply $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ on top. You paid triple to compute an object you did not need.
- **Accuracy.** Every entry of the explicit $A^{-1}$ is itself a computed, rounded number;
  multiplying $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ then accumulates *those* rounding errors on top of the
  arithmetic. Direct solving keeps the original $A$ (whose entries are exact data) in play
  longer and provably produces a smaller error. The gap widens precisely when $A$ is
  ill-conditioned — exactly when you can least afford it.
- **Sparsity.** If $A$ is large and *sparse* (mostly zeros — the norm for graphs, grids, and
  big regressions), its inverse is almost always **dense** (mostly nonzero). Forming
  $A^{-1}$ can turn a matrix you could store in megabytes into one that needs terabytes. A
  direct solve preserves and exploits the sparsity.

So the rule **"solve, don't invert"** is not folklore: $A^{-1}$ is the *name for the
backwards map*, a beautiful theoretical object (and we needed it to *prove* @eq-solve and
@thm-invertible), but you rarely want to *build* it. When you see $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ in a
formula — including the $(X^\top X)^{-1}X^\top\mathbf{y}$ of Regression R5 — read it as "the
$\mathbf{x}$ solving $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$," and compute it with a solver.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Your derivation literally produced $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ — so why do you tell me
not to compute $A^{-1}$?"* Because $A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ is a *mathematical expression*, not an
*algorithm*. The expression is correct and indispensable for reasoning (it is how we proved
existence, uniqueness, and the product/transpose identities). But the cheapest, most
accurate way to *evaluate* that expression is to solve the system, never materializing the
matrix $A^{-1}$. The distinction between an expression and the algorithm that evaluates it is
exactly what a strong interviewer is checking — and it generalizes: you also never form
$(X^\top X)^{-1}$ in regression; you solve the normal equations (better still, you skip them
and use QR or SVD on $X$ directly, because forming $X^\top X$ squares the condition number,
[GEOM-26]).
:::

## When is the inverse the right tool at all? {#sec-when-inverse}

A compact applicability summary, the kind you should be able to recite:

- **Use the (concept of the) inverse** to *reason*: to know whether a square system has a
  unique solution, to manipulate matrix equations symbolically, to state results like
  @eq-lsq cleanly. The inverse is the right *idea* whenever you are asking "can this map be
  undone, and is the answer unique."
- **Actually solve, never invert,** to *compute*: use `numpy.linalg.solve` /
  `scipy.linalg.solve` (or a structure-aware Cholesky/QR/LU) rather than `inv`. The only
  time forming $A^{-1}$ is justified is when you genuinely need *the whole inverse matrix as
  an object* — e.g. the full covariance matrix of regression estimates, $\sigma^2(X^\top
  X)^{-1}$, where every entry is wanted — and even then you compute it from a factorization,
  not by `inv`.
- **Reach for the pseudoinverse** ([GEOM-21]) when $A$ is rectangular or rank-deficient, i.e.
  the no-solution or infinitely-many cases: it gives the least-squares (and minimum-norm)
  answer that the plain inverse cannot.
- **Reach for an iterative solver** (conjugate gradient and friends, owned by the
  Optimization track) when $A$ is enormous and sparse and even one direct factorization is
  too expensive; these never touch $A^{-1}$ either.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the read-as-a-pair cluster **[GEOM-08]–[GEOM-10]**: a matrix is a map
([GEOM-08]), composing maps is matrix multiplication ([GEOM-09]), and the transpose is the
adjoint that slides across a dot product ([GEOM-10]). This article asked the inverse
question — *can the map be run backwards?* — and answered it three ways. From here the trunk
continues, and every promissory note we left ("full rank," "$\det\neq 0$," "project onto the
column space," "minimum-norm solution," "condition number") is discharged downstream:

- **[GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four Fundamental Subspaces*]** — names the column space and null
  space we used informally, proves the rank–nullity theorem (which makes the
  existence/uniqueness count exact for *rectangular* $A$), and shows how the four subspaces
  pair $A$ with $A^\top$.
- **[GEOM-13 *The Determinant*]** — the single number that flags singularity (condition in
  @thm-invertible), as signed volume.
- **[GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection*]** — the keystone behind the no-solution repair of
  @sec-no-solution; derives why "drop a perpendicular" is the closest point and where the
  normal equations @eq-normal-eq come from.
- **[GEOM-19 *Cholesky*]** and **[GEOM-21 *The Pseudoinverse*]** — the stable, always-defined
  replacements for $A^{-1}$ in the well-posed and the ill-/over-/under-posed cases
  respectively.
- **[GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]** — makes the "invertible but
  untrustworthy" edge of @sec-ill-conditioned quantitative, and proves the "solve, don't
  invert" rule's numerical half.
- **Downstream of the track:** this article directly feeds **Regression R5**, where the
  multiple-regression normal equations $X^\top X\hat{\boldsymbol\beta}=X^\top\mathbf{y}$ and
  the object $(X^\top X)^{-1}$ are precisely @eq-normal-eq and @eq-lsq specialized to a
  design matrix.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **The original algorithm.** *Jiuzhang Suanshu* (*The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical
  Art*), chapter 8 *Fangcheng*, ~200 BCE — Gaussian elimination two thousand years before
  Gauss. A modern annotated translation is Shen, Crossley & Lun, *The Nine Chapters on the
  Mathematical Art* (Oxford, 1999).
- **The modern geometric telling.** Gilbert Strang, *Introduction to Linear Algebra* — the
  "column picture vs. row picture" framing of @sec-columns-reading and the four-subspaces
  view this article previews are his pedagogical signature.
- **The numerical reality.** Trefethen & Bau, *Numerical Linear Algebra* (1997) — the
  authoritative source for "solve, don't invert," conditioning, and why factorizations beat
  explicit inverses. Lecture 1's epigraph is essentially @sec-solve-dont-invert.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *How many solutions exactly, for a
  rectangular $A$?* — the rank–nullity count, [GEOM-12]. *What is the "closest point"
  rigorously, and why orthogonal?* — [GEOM-06]. *How do we solve safely when $A$ is nearly
  singular?* — factorizations and conditioning, [GEOM-19], [GEOM-26]. *What is the one object
  that solves every shape and rank at once?* — the SVD-built pseudoinverse, [GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21].
